description: The provided image is an irrelevant non-scientific functional button, which displays the text 'Check for updates', an incomplete circular icon with blue upper half and yellow lower half, and a red bookmark shape inside the ring. It does not correspond to any figure content of the provided low density δ-TRIP steel research paper, so no valid material scientific information related to microstructure, mechanical properties, heat treatment processes of the studied Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C steel can be extracted from this image.

This figure is a temperature-time schematic diagram of the full thermomechanical and heat treatment process route for the experimental Fe–9Mn–5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel. First, the ingot undergoes homogenization at 1100 °C for 2 h, followed by multi-pass hot rolling, then air cooling to room temperature to produce hot-rolled samples. Next, the hot-rolled plates are reheated to 1050 °C (the ferrite-austenite dual-phase region) and held for 1 h, then water quenched to obtain intercritically quenched samples. Finally, the quenched samples are annealed for 1 h at temperatures of 620 °C, 680 °C, 740 °C, or 800 °C, followed by air cooling to room temperature. The critical A_c1 phase transformation temperature of the steel is marked as 617 °C. This schematic clearly documents key processing parameters for each step, supporting the study's investigation of annealing temperature effects on the steel's microstructure and mechanical properties.

This figure contains two subplots of thermodynamic calculation results for Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel obtained via Thermo-Calc software with the TCFE 12 database. Subplot (a) plots the molar amount of four phases (FCC_A1 austenite, BCC_A2 δ-ferrite, cementite, liquid) as a function of temperature from room temperature to 1600 °C. Key features include: cementite precipitates below 617 °C, austenite fraction reaches a maximum of ~0.914 mol at ~970 °C, δ-ferrite precipitates from liquid at ~1485 °C, and austenite forms via peritectic reaction at ~1390 °C. Subplot (b) shows the mass fraction of Mn, Al, and C in ferrite and austenite as functions of temperature, revealing that Mn and C contents in austenite increase significantly below 970 °C until cementite precipitation at 617 °C, while Al content is higher in ferrite than in austenite. This calculation supports selection of 1050 °C as the intercritical quenching temperature and 620/680/740/800 °C as annealing temperatures, as high Al addition expands the δ-ferrite region so full austenitization cannot be achieved, and a wide ferrite-austenite dual-phase region exists.

This group of 8 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs illustrates the microstructures of Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel under different heat treatment conditions. Subgraphs (a) (50μm scale bar) and (b) (10μm scale bar) correspond to the hot-rolled (HR) state, showing a laminated structure composed of δ-ferrite (δ-F) bands, lath martensite (M), retained austenite (RA), and α-ferrite (α-F). Subgraphs (c) (50μm scale bar) and (d) (10μm scale bar) correspond to the intercritically quenched state at 1050 °C (IQ-1050), retaining the laminated structure with thinner, discontinuous δ-F bands, larger martensite regions, less RA, and visible prior austenite grain boundaries (PAGB). Subgraphs (e) to (h) (5μm scale bar for main view, 1μm for inset of (e) and (f)) correspond to intercritically quenched samples annealed at 620 °C (QA-620, with carbide (Cm) precipitates in tempered martensite), 680 °C (QA-680), 740 °C (QA-740, fine reverted austenite grains between tempered martensite laths), and 800 °C (QA-800, partial austenite transformed to fresh martensite during air cooling). The micrographs demonstrate that heat treatment parameters, especially annealing temperature, significantly adjust phase composition, morphology, and austenite stability, providing the microstructural basis for optimizing mechanical properties of the δ-TRIP steel.

This set of 6 electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) band contrast (BC) maps with red color-marked austenite display the microstructures of Fe–9Mn–5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel under different processing conditions: (a) HR (hot-rolled state), (b) IQ-1050 (intercritically quenched at 1050 °C), (c) QA-620, (d) QA-680, (e) QA-740, (f) QA-800 (IQ-1050 samples annealed at 620 °C, 680 °C, 740 °C, 800 °C respectively for 1 h followed by air cooling, 50 μm scale bar applies to all subgraphs). Brighter white regions are δ-ferrite bands, darker regions correspond to lath martensite (for HR and IQ-1050) or tempered martensite (for QA series). Austenite is concentrated in HR, very scarce in IQ-1050, and widely distributed as fine grains in the tempered martensite matrix of QA specimens. Austenite volume fraction increases with annealing temperature rising from 620 °C to 740 °C, while no obvious increment even slight reduction occurs at 800 °C because partial austenite transforms to martensite during air cooling. This figure confirms that annealing temperature can effectively regulate the content and distribution of austenite to optimize the mechanical properties of the tested δ-TRIP steel.

This figure consists of two subplots for mechanical property characterization of Fe–9Mn–5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel with different heat treatments. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves for hot-rolled (HR), 1050°C intercritical quenched (IQ-1050), and intercritical quenching + annealed (QA, annealed at 620°C, 680°C, 740°C, 800°C for 1h respectively) specimens. HR and IQ-1050 samples show high tensile strength (~1107 MPa and ~929 MPa respectively) but very low elongation (<6%), while all QA samples exhibit greatly improved ductility, with QA-740 achieving the highest total elongation of ~70% and ultimate tensile strength of ~725 MPa. Subplot (b) presents work hardening rate curves of QA samples versus true strain. Work hardening rate drops sharply at initial true strain ε<0.01, then decreases slowly, with almost overlapping curves for all QA samples when ε<0.15. QA-620 shows higher work hardening rate than other QA samples at 0.01<ε<0.1, attributed to carbide precipitates retarding dislocation glide, while work hardening rate differences for ε>0.15 correspond to varying TRIP effect levels at different annealing temperatures. This figure confirms that proper intercritical quenching-annealing treatment significantly improves the comprehensive mechanical performance of the studied δ-TRIP steel, with 740°C annealing delivering the optimal strength-ductility combination.

This figure presents four scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs of tensile fracture surfaces of Fe–9Mn–5Al-0.3C low density δ-TRIP steel samples subjected to intercritical quenching at 1050 °C followed by 1 h annealing at different temperatures: (a) QA-620 (620 °C annealed), (b) QA-680 (680 °C annealed), (c) QA-740 (740 °C annealed), (d) QA-800 (800 °C annealed). All samples show mixed fracture features: red dashed regions mark cleavage areas with river patterns and cleavage steps (brittle fracture of δ-ferrite), blue dashed regions mark quasi-cleavage areas, yellow dashed regions mark dimples (ductile fracture feature), while second cracks and laminar tears are also observed. QA-620 exhibits smaller micro-dimples than other samples; QA-680 and QA-740 show crisscrossed cleavage/quasi-cleavage and dimple regions with extra larger dimples; QA-800 presents alternating cleavage layers dominated by cleavage steps and dimple layers. These morphology differences match the varied mechanical properties of samples annealed at different temperatures, supporting the effect of annealing temperature on fracture behavior of the studied steel.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results for Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel subjected to six different thermomechanical/heat treatment processes: hot-rolled (HR), intercritically quenched at 1050 °C (IQ-1050), and IQ followed by 1 h annealing at 620 °C (QA-620), 680 °C (QA-680), 740 °C (QA-740), and 800 °C (QA-800). Each sample group includes three subgraphs: (a, b, c, d, e, f) are band contrast (BC) maps focusing on (tempered) martensite morphology, with a uniform scale bar of 50 μm; (a-1 to f-1) are corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps, used to characterize geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) density, with the color scale ranging from blue (low misorientation, low GND density) to green (high misorientation, high GND density); (a-2 to f-2) are grain boundary distribution maps, where black lines denote austenite grain (AG) boundaries (15°–45°), blue lines high-angle packet boundaries, cyan lines low-angle packet boundaries, red lines block boundaries (>45°), and green lines sub-block boundaries. Key observations include: HR has a distinct banded laminated structure with the highest GND density; as annealing temperature increases for QA samples, block boundary density continuously decreases, while AG boundary density first increases then drops at 800 °C due to austenite grain coarsening, and GND density gradually reduces, corresponding to the decreasing yield strength and first increasing then decreasing elongation (QA-740 achieves the maximum elongation of 62.4%). These EBSD results reveal the microstructure, dislocation, and grain boundary evolution regulated by heat treatment, providing microstructural evidence for the mechanical property variation of the tested steel.

This figure consists of two subplots characterizing microstructure parameters of Fe–9Mn–5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel under six processing conditions: hot rolled (HR), intercritical quenched at 1050 °C (IQ-1050), and IQ samples annealed at 620, 680, 740, 800 °C (QA-620 to QA-800). Subplot (a) is a line plot of geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) density (unit: ×10^14 m⁻²) for BCC and FCC phases. Both phases show a continuous decreasing trend of GND density as annealing temperature rises: BCC GND density reduces from 10.81×10^14 m⁻² (HR) to 4.63×10^14 m⁻² (QA-800), while FCC GND density falls from 8.37×10^14 m⁻² (HR) to 2.66×10^14 m⁻² (QA-800). Subplot (b) is a bar chart of grain boundary density (unit: μm⁻¹) for five boundary types, where austenitic grain (AG, 15°-45°) and block (>45°) boundaries are the dominant types. For QA series, AG boundary density first increases with annealing temperature to a peak at QA-740 then decreases, while block boundary density continuously declines with rising annealing temperature. The results support that decreasing block boundary density contributes to the yield strength reduction of annealed samples as annealing temperature increases.

This figure contains three subplots characterizing Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel specimens treated via intercritical quenching plus annealing at 620 °C (QA-620), 680 °C (QA-680), 740 °C (QA-740), and 800 °C (QA-800). Subplot (a) displays XRD patterns of the four QA samples before and after tensile tests, with labeled characteristic diffraction peaks for FCC austenite (γ) and BCC α (ferrite/martensite) phases. Subplot (b) is a bar chart comparing austenitic volume fractions before and after tensile deformation: all specimens show reduced austenite fraction after tension, proving the occurrence of transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) effect during deformation. Subplot (c) shows dot-line plots of austenitic mechanical stability constant k and stacking fault energy (SFE): k reaches the minimum value of 0.56 for QA-680 (highest austenite stability), while k values of QA-740 and QA-800 are 1.47 and 1.50 respectively; SFE first rises to a maximum of 38.27 mJ/m² for QA-680 then drops to 31.53 mJ/m² for QA-800, presenting an opposite trend to k, which verifies that higher SFE corresponds to higher austenitic mechanical stability. The figure demonstrates that annealing temperature regulates austenite stability and deformation mechanisms of the tested steel by adjusting SFE and austenite fraction.

This set of 4 subplots presents work hardening rate (WHR, red lines, y-axis unit MPa) and true stress-strain (black lines) curves for four intercritically quenched and annealed (QA) Fe–9Mn–5Al-0.3C low-density δ-TRIP steel samples: QA-620 (annealed at 620°C), QA-680 (680°C), QA-740 (740°C), QA-800 (800°C). Yellow points mark the necking point for each sample. All WHR curves share a three-stage characteristic: Stage I shows a sharp WHR decrease, attributed to dislocation gliding in constituent phases with different hardness; Stage II features a slower WHR decline, arising from the competition between hardening effects (grain boundary dislocation hindrance, TRIP effect) and WHR-reducing mechanisms (dislocation glide, annihilation, cross-slip); Stage III shows relatively stable or slightly rising WHR before necking. The necking strain increases with annealing temperature up to 740°C (QA-740 has the highest necking strain ~0.5, matching its highest measured elongation of 62.4%), then decreases for QA-800. This figure demonstrates the correlation between annealing temperature-modulated microstructure, deformation mechanisms, and tensile mechanical performance of the studied steels.

This set of four histograms presents austenitic grain size statistics (calculated via equivalent circle method from EBSD data) of four quenching-annealing (QA) treated Fe–9Mn–5Al-0.3C low density δ-TRIP steel specimens before tensile tests, corresponding to samples annealed at 620 °C (QA-620, subplot a), 680 °C (QA-680, subplot b), 740 °C (QA-740, subplot c) and 800 °C (QA-800, subplot d) respectively. The x-axis represents austenitic grain size intervals with unit μm, and the y-axis represents the proportion of grains in each interval with unit %. All four samples show a similar distribution trend: the proportion of grains increases as grain size decreases, with around 90% of austenite grains smaller than 4.8 μm. Finer austenite grains possess higher alloy enrichment and higher stability, which directly affects the TRIP effect during deformation, thus regulating the work hardening behavior and mechanical properties of the steel. This figure supports the conclusion that annealing temperature modulates austenite grain size and stability to optimize the comprehensive mechanical performance of the studied δ-TRIP steel.
